notes: Walter George Wolfe (1937-2020) received his MD from Temple University (1963) and entered the Duke general and cardiothoracic surgery residency in 1964, becoming one of the earliest residents under Sabiston's newly established chairmanship. He completed training in 1971 after a research fellowship at UCSF with J.B. West. Sabiston appointed him to the Duke faculty in 1972, where he served for over 40 years as professor of surgery and biomedical engineering, chief of cardiothoracic surgery at the Durham VA, and thoracic residency program director (1994-2002). He was Sabiston's primary surgical partner for pulmonary embolism cases. Designated Duke Master Surgeon in 2015. Chitwood (Ann Surg 2003) documents the Sabiston training heritage comprehensively. TARGET MODULE: 02_general_surgery_spread.json

Ralph Randal Bollinger (1944-) received his MD from Tulane and was accepted in 1970 into the Duke general surgical residency under Sabiston while simultaneously pursuing a PhD in immunology with D. Bernard Amos. Residency was interrupted by 2 years of USAF service. Sabiston appointed him to the Duke surgery faculty in 1980 as chief of transplantation (1983). Bollinger performed the first liver transplant in North Carolina (1986) and mentored Allan D. Kirk (future Duke surgery chair) and Stuart Knechtle in his transplant immunology laboratory. The Bollinger-Kirk lineage creates a verified 4-generation chain: Blalock-Sabiston-Bollinger-Kirk. Kirk et al (Front Transplant 2024) document Bollinger's training under Sabiston explicitly. TARGET MODULE: 02_general_surgery_spread.json

Robert W. Anderson was a Duke general and cardiothoracic surgery resident under Sabiston who subsequently returned to Duke as Sabiston's successor and chairman of the Department of Surgery (1994-2003). The Duke Department of Surgery history page confirms Anderson 'returned to the site of his surgical training.' Exact residency dates are not published in the available literature; the range c.1968-c.1976 is estimated based on his career timeline (chair at age consistent with 1970s completion). D'Amico (JTCVS 2015, PMID 25135240) identifies Anderson as Sabiston's direct successor. Anderson was a cardiothoracic surgeon with an MBA who led Duke Surgery through major practice reimbursement changes. Confidence moderate due to estimated dates. TARGET MODULE: 02_general_surgery_spread.json

Anderson succeeded Sabiston as chairman of the Duke Department of Surgery in 1994 and served until 2003. As a Sabiston trainee who returned to lead the department, Anderson creates a bidirectional circuit (Sabiston trained Anderson at Duke; Anderson led Duke after Sabiston) — a pattern seen elsewhere in the graph with Gross at Jefferson and Altemeier at Cincinnati. The Duke succession chain is now Hart (1930-1960) - Gardner (1960-1964) - Sabiston (1964-1994) - Anderson (1994-2003) - Jacobs (2003-2012) - Kirk (2014-2025) - Allen (2025-present). TARGET MODULE: 02_general_surgery_spread.json

W. Randolph Chitwood Jr. trained in general and cardiothoracic surgery at Duke under Sabiston and subsequently became Professor and Chairman of the Department of Surgery at the Brody School of Medicine, East Carolina University (Greenville, NC). He authored the definitive review of Sabiston's educational legacy (Ann Surg 2003, PMID 14703738), which documents Sabiston's training of 154 chief residents with 41 becoming department chairs or division chiefs. Chitwood was a pioneer in robotic mitral valve surgery and served as founding member and president of the Sabiston Society. Exact residency dates approximated from career timeline. TARGET MODULE: 02_general_surgery_spread.json

James L. Cox trained at Duke under Sabiston, rising to associate professor of surgery in 1983 before being recruited to Washington University in St. Louis as professor and chief of cardiothoracic surgery at age 40. At Duke, Cox established a surgical electrophysiology laboratory where he began the research that led to the maze procedure for atrial fibrillation — one of the most impactful surgical innovations of the late 20th century. He became the 81st president of the AATS. D'Amico (JTCVS 2015, PMID 25135240) lists Cox as one of four AATS presidents trained by Sabiston (alongside Ebert, Crawford, and Spray). The Cox AATS Historical Perspectives article (JTCVS 2013) confirms his Duke training under Sabiston and laboratory work. TARGET MODULE: 02_general_surgery_spread.json

Thomas L. Spray (b. 1948) was accepted into the Duke surgery pyramid program's early internship track and trained under Sabiston alongside colleagues including Cox, Crawford, and Ebert. He became the 89th president of the AATS. D'Amico (JTCVS 2015, PMID 25135240) identifies Spray as one of four subsequent AATS presidents trained by Sabiston. The Spray AATS Historical Perspectives article (JTCVS 2016) describes Duke's pyramid residency program and Sabiston's influence in detail. Spray became chief of cardiothoracic surgery at Children's Hospital of Philadelphia (CHOP). Exact residency dates approximated from birth year and career timeline. TARGET MODULE: 02_general_surgery_spread.json

Robert H. Jones received his MD from Johns Hopkins University in 1965 and was 'one of the first Duke University surgical residents taught by Dr. David C. Sabiston, Jr.' per the Duke Department of Surgery memorial. His residency was interrupted by military service as a Captain in the Air Force. He joined the Duke faculty as assistant professor in 1975 and spent 40 years at Duke as a cardiac surgeon, clinical investigator, and resident educator. Jones was a Howard Hughes Investigator who made seminal contributions in nuclear cardiology imaging and was instrumental in developing the Duke Cardiovascular Disease Databank. No published PMID specifically documents his residency dates; the Duke Department of Surgery memorial page is the primary source. TARGET MODULE: 02_general_surgery_spread.json
